skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for designing, implementing, and evaluating Model Context Protocol servers for external APIs in Python or Node/TypeScript.
skill.summary: Build well-designed MCP servers with implementation guides, evaluation workflows, and security-conscious tool design.
skill.author: ComposioHQ

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity hits, but most are false positives from markdown examples, license text, and instructional code snippets. The confirmed risk is the included evaluation workflow: it can launch user-supplied MCP commands, pass environment values, connect to remote endpoints, and send tool data to Anthropic during evaluation. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Harness Runs User-Supplied Commands
medium_findings.description: The evaluation CLI accepts a stdio command, arguments, and environment values, then passes them into MCP stdio server parameters. This is legitimate for testing local MCP servers, but it can execute arbitrary local commands if a user follows untrusted instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and arguments are explicit CLI inputs and are passed directly into the MCP stdio client. The purpose is legitimate evaluation, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Data Is Sent to Anthropic API
medium_findings.description: The harness sends evaluation questions, tool schemas, and tool results to the Anthropic Messages API. This can expose sensitive tool outputs if users run evaluations against production systems or private data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly creates Anthropic messages and includes tool results in follow-up messages. Data exposure depends on what server and dataset the user evaluates.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Connections Can Include User-Supplied Headers
medium_findings.description: The evaluation CLI supports SSE and HTTP URLs with custom headers. This is expected for remote MCP testing, but users can send authorization headers to arbitrary endpoints if they choose an untrusted URL.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The remote URL and headers are user-controlled and forwarded to MCP clients. The behavior is intentional, but it expands the risk surface for credential handling.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to MCP and SDK documentation that the skill asks users to fetch. These are external network references, but they are transparent documentation sources rather than hidden endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in setup instructions and point to public documentation. No hidden exfiltration endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Many weak cryptography blocker hits occur in license text, markdown headings, and reference prose rather than cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or weak cipher use in executable logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged locations and found legal prose or markdown documentation. The scanner appears to match text patterns without semantic code context.

low_findings.title: Instructional Code Examples Trigger Broad Static Alerts
low_findings.description: The reference guides include many example commands, API keys, environment variables, and HTTP client imports. These examples are expected for an MCP-building guide and are not automatically executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is in markdown reference material and setup instructions. It documents patterns users may implement, rather than executing during skill loading.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local Process Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio evaluation path launches a command selected by the user. Users should run this only for trusted MCP server commands in isolated development environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command flow is clear and direct. The risk is real when copied into unsafe workflows, although it is not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Model Submission of Tool Results
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluation loop forwards tool output to Anthropic as part of model conversations. Test data should be synthetic or approved for third-party processing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API call and tool-result serialization are explicit. Whether it becomes sensitive depends on user-provided server output.

content.value_statement: MCP server builders need more than endpoint wrappers to create useful agent tools. This skill provides planning, implementation, and evaluation guidance for Python and Node/TypeScript MCP servers.
actual_capabilities: Explains agent-centered MCP tool design and workflow planning.
Provides Python FastMCP implementation guidance with Pydantic validation patterns.
Provides Node and TypeScript implementation guidance with Zod validation patterns.
Guides MCP server testing with syntax checks, builds, and evaluation tasks.
Includes an evaluation harness for running question-answer tests against MCP servers.
Documents response formatting, pagination, tool annotations, and error handling practices.
limitations: It is a guide and helper script package, not a complete generated MCP server.
Users must verify current MCP SDK behavior and API documentation before release.
The evaluation harness can run local commands and contact external services when invoked.
Generated MCP servers still require manual review for authentication, authorization, and data handling.

use_cases.title: Plan a New API MCP Server
use_cases.description: Identify useful workflows, define tool boundaries, and plan shared request, pagination, and error handling utilities.

use_cases.title: Implement Language-Specific MCP Tools
use_cases.description: Apply Python FastMCP or TypeScript SDK patterns for validated inputs, clear tool descriptions, and useful responses.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Agent Tool Quality
use_cases.description: Create realistic evaluation questions and run a harness to measure whether agents can complete tasks with the server.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Initial Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to help me plan an MCP server for this API. Identify the highest-value workflows, proposed tool names, and required shared utilities.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tool Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to design validated input schemas and response formats for these MCP tools. Include pagination, concise output options, and actionable errors.

prompt_templates.title: Review an MCP Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to review this MCP server implementation for tool quality, type safety, response shape, error handling, and agent usability.

prompt_templates.title: Create Evaluation Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to create evaluation scenarios for this MCP server. Focus on realistic read-only questions with stable, verifiable answers.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output_examples.output: A prioritized tool plan with workflow-focused operations, shared API helpers, pagination rules, and validation requirements.

output_examples.input: Review my TypeScript MCP server before release.
output_examples.output: A review covering tool naming, Zod schema quality, response formats, build checks, and gaps that affect agent usability.

output_examples.input: Create evaluation questions for a read-only CRM MCP server.
output_examples.output: A set of realistic tasks with stable expected answers and guidance for running them through the included harness.

best_practices: Design tools around complete user workflows instead of one-to-one API endpoint wrappers.
Use strict input validation, clear descriptions, and concise response formats for every tool.
Run evaluations with safe test data and review any data sent to model providers.
anti_patterns: Do not expose destructive operations without clear annotations, confirmation flows, and authorization checks.
Do not return large unfiltered API responses when a concise task-focused answer is enough.
Do not run the evaluation harness against untrusted servers with real secrets or sensitive data.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design, implement, and evaluate MCP servers that connect agents to external APIs and services.

faq.question: Does it support both Python and TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes separate guidance for Python FastMCP and the TypeScript MCP SDK.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete server automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a structured development process, reference patterns, and evaluation scripts for your implementation work.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention command execution?
faq.answer: The included evaluation harness can start a local MCP server command supplied by the user. Run it only with trusted commands.

faq.question: What data should I avoid using in evaluations?
faq.answer: Avoid real credentials, customer data, private files, and production records because evaluation output can be sent to a model provider.
